arguments.command: which llvm-symbolizer addr2line llvm-objdump 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; apt list --installed 2>/dev/null | grep -E 'llvm|binutils' | head -5

data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to symbolicate the .NET runtime frames in this Android tombstone. The key libraries are:

1. `libmonosgen-2.0.so` (BuildId: `1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be`) - frames #01, #02, #03, #06
2. `libSystem.Native.so` (BuildId: `7a3b5c9d1e4f6082a3c5d7e9f1b2d4a6c8e0f213`) - frames #04, #07


3. `libSystem.Globalization.Native.so` (BuildId: `2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e`) - frame #05

To get the symbols, I'll need to query Microsoft's symbol server using the BuildIds to retrieve the debug information for each library.

For ELF files, the symbol server URL format uses the BuildId in a specific pattern. I should try fetching the symbol files directly from the server, or alternatively use tools like `llvm-symbolizer` or `addr2line` if I have the symbol files locally. I should try using `llvm-symbolizer` or `addr2line` to resolve the symbols, and I have the build IDs for the three libraries I need to work with. Looking at these build IDs, some appear to follow regular patterns that suggest they might be fabricated test data, while others look more genuinely random. Let me try fetching the symbols to see what actually exists, and if these turn out to be fictional, I'll let you know. First, I should check what symbolication tools are available on the system.

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.command:
mkdir -p /tmp/symbols && cd /tmp/symbols

# Try downloading from Microsoft symbol server
# Format: https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/<filename>/elf-buildid-<buildid>/<filename>

# libmonosgen-2.0.so (BuildId: 1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be)
curl -sfSL -o libmonosgen-2.0.so "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so/elf-buildid-1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be/libmonosgen-2.0.so" -w "\nHTTP %{http_code} libmonosgen\n" 2>&1 &

# libSystem.Native.so (BuildId: 7a3b5c9d1e4f6082a3c5d7e9f1b2d4a6c8e0f213)
curl -sfSL -o libSystem.Native.so "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libSystem.Native.so/elf-buildid-7a3b5c9d1e4f6082a3c5d7e9f1b2d4a6c8e0f213/libSystem.Native.so" -w "\nHTTP %{http_code} libSystem.Native\n" 2>&1 &

# libSystem.Globalization.Native.so (BuildId: 2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e)
curl -sfSL -o libSystem.Globalization.Native.so "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libSystem.Globalization.Native.so/elf-buildid-2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e/libSystem.Globalization.Native.so" -w "\nHTTP %{http_code} libSystem.Globalization.Native\n" 2>&1 &

wait
echo "---downloads done---"
ls -la /tmp/symbols/
arguments.description: Download symbols from Microsoft symbol server

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Download symbols from Microsoft symbol server

data.reasoningText:
Good, I have `addr2line` and `binutils-aarch64-linux-gnu` installed. Now I need to download the symbol files from the Microsoft symbol server. Let me try fetching the libraries using their BuildIds.

The Microsoft symbol server URL format for ELF files is typically:
`https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/<filename>/elf-buildid-<buildid>/<filename>`

Let me try downloading the libraries. I'll need to use the aarch64 addr2line since these are arm64 binaries.

arguments.command:
mkdir -p /tmp/symbols && cd /tmp/symbols

# Try downloading from Microsoft symbol server
# Format: https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/<filename>/elf-buildid-<buildid>/<filename>

# libmonosgen-2.0.so (BuildId: 1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be)
curl -sfSL -o libmonosgen-2.0.so "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so/elf-buildid-1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be/libmonosgen-2.0.so" -w "\nHTTP %{http_code} libmonosgen\n" 2>&1 &

# libSystem.Native.so (BuildId: 7a3b5c9d1e4f6082a3c5d7e9f1b2d4a6c8e0f213)
curl -sfSL -o libSystem.Native.so "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libSystem.Native.so/elf-buildid-7a3b5c9d1e4f6082a3c5d7e9f1b2d4a6c8e0f213/libSystem.Native.so" -w "\nHTTP %{http_code} libSystem.Native\n" 2>&1 &

# libSystem.Globalization.Native.so (BuildId: 2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e)
curl -sfSL -o libSystem.Globalization.Native.so "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libSystem.Globalization.Native.so/elf-buildid-2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e/libSystem.Globalization.Native.so" -w "\nHTTP %{http_code} libSystem.Globalization.Native\n" 2>&1 &

wait
echo "---downloads done---"
ls -la /tmp/symbols/

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.command:
cd /tmp/symbols

# Check if the downloaded binary has symbols
file libmonosgen-2.0.so
aarch64-linux-gnu-readelf -S libmonosgen-2.0.so | grep -E 'debug|symtab|strtab' | head -10

# Also try downloading debug symbols
curl -sfSL -o libmonosgen-2.0.so.dbg "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be/_.debug" -w "\nHTTP %{http_code}\n" 2>&1 &
curl -sfSL -o libmonosgen-2.0.so.dbg2 "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so.dbg/elf-buildid-sym-1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be/libmonosgen-2.0.so.dbg" -w "\nHTTP %{http_code}\n" 2>&1 &
wait
ls -la *.dbg*
arguments.description: Check symbols and download debug info

arguments.command:
cd /tmp/symbols

# Symbolicate the monosgen frames using addr2line
echo "=== libmonosgen-2.0.so frames ==="
for addr in 0x222098 0x222234 0x19bac0 0x19bc14; do
  echo -n "pc $addr -> "
  aarch64-linux-gnu-addr2line -f -e libmonosgen-2.0.so.dbg "$addr" 2>&1
  echo "---"
done

data.reasoningText:
The `.dbg` file is a valid ELF with debug info. The main `.so` file from the symbol server seems to be corrupted or has a different format. But I have the debug symbols file which is what I need for symbolication. Let me use `aarch64-linux-gnu-addr2line` with the debug file to resolve the PC offsets.

The frames for libmonosgen-2.0.so are:
- #01 pc 0x222098
- #02 pc 0x222234
- #03 pc 0x19bac0
- #06 pc 0x19bc14


Now I'll run addr2line against the debug file to get the actual function names and source locations for these addresses.
